edibility: Inedible — the hard, woody, charcoal-like fruiting body cannot be consumed. Historically used as tinder for fire-starting with flint and steel. Folklore suggests a use for alleviating cramps, though no medicinal efficacy is established.
taste: Not consumed; the extremely hard fruiting body would be impossible to chew or digest.
search: Found on dead hardwoods, particularly ash and beech trees. Forms distinctive black, hemispherical balls 2-7 cm wide that resemble charcoal briquettes. Identification is confirmed by the distinctive concentric rings visible when the fruiting body is cut in half. Similar-looking fungi are rare, but any hard black fruiting body on wood should be examined for the interior structure.

toxicity.antidote: N/A — not toxic if accidentally ingested
